coredata.dc:title: Enhanced reduction of p-nitrophenol by zerovalent iron modified with carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Peng
Zhang, Xuanjun
Deng, Huanhuan
Guan, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: The pollution of aromatic nitro compounds in water is a critical issue, and the reduction of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) by the combination of catalyst/reductant has been a hot topic in recent years. The methods reported previously bore several defects, such as high cost for material synthesis and secondary pollution to the environment. Herein, this study employed the non-toxic and inexpensive materials, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) as the catalyst and zerovalent iron (ZVI) as the reductant, to prepare the ZVI modified with C-dots (C-dots@ZVI) for p-NP removal in water under anaerobic condition. The results showed that the removal rate of p-NP, the capacity, and the selectivity of ZVI towards p-NP removal were greatly improved, due to the involvement of C-dots. The rate constant of p-NP removal by C-dots@ZVI was 1192.7 folds of that by ZVI. The capacity of p-NP removal by C-dots@ZVI at 24.0 h was 24.8 orders of magnitude higher than that of ZVI. At the end of 2.0 h reaction with p-NP, the utilization ratio and electron efficiency of C-dots@ZVI were increased by 3.7 times and 2.9 times, respectively, compared to those of ZVI. The enhancing effect of C-dots on p-NP sequestration by ZVI should be mainly associated with the promoted hydrophobicity of ZVI surface, the facilitated electron transfer, and the accelerated corrosion of ZVI. As an innovative catalyst/reductant system for p-NP reduction, C-dots@ZVI is effective, facile, low-cost, and environmental-friendly.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zerovalent iron
Carbon quantum dots
Kinetics
Removal capacity
Electron efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The performance of C-dots@ZVI for p-NP reduction
Mechanism of the enhanced p-NP reduction by C-dots@ZVI

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China
State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the fabrication procedure of carbon quantum dots (C-dots) and zerovalent iron modified with C-dots (C-dots@ZVI).

(a) Excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of C-dots solution (Insets: Photographs of C-dots solution under the irradiations of visible (left) and ultraviolet (right) light); (b) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of C-dots (Inset: High-resolution TEM of C-dots); (c) Scanning electron microscope (SEM) image and energy-dispersive-spectrometer (EDS) mapping of C-dots@ZVI; (d) C 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of ZVI and C-dots@ZVI.

(a) Kinetics of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) removal by different ZVI materials and C-dots alone; (b) Summary of the pseudo-first-order rate constants (k obs) of p-NP removal by different ZVI materials; (c) Variations of p-aminophenol (p-AP) concentration in different systems; (d) The variations of the sum of p-NP and p-AP concentrations in different systems. Reaction conditions: ZVI material =2.0 g/L, [p-NP]0 = 50.0 mg/L, pH0 5.0, T =25 °C, rotary rate =25 rpm, under anaerobic condition.

Kinetics of p-NP removal by ZVI and C-dots@ZVI in consecutive runs. Reaction conditions: ZVI material =2.0 g/L, 50.0 mg/L p-NP was dosed to the mixture before the initiation of each run, pH0 5.0, T =25 °C, rotary rate =25 rpm, under anaerobic condition.

(a) Water contact-angles of different ZVI materials; (b) Summary of the corrosion potentials (E corr), the corrosion current densities (i corr), and the polarization resistances (R p) of the working electrode modified with different ZVI materials; (c) Fe k 3-weighted extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra of ZVI and C-dots@ZVI at 0 h and 2.0 h (Experimental data are shown as the dashed lines and the solid lines represent the linear combination fitting results); (d) Summary of the electron efficiencies (EE) and utilization ratios (UR) of different ZVI materials after reacting with p-NP solution for 2.0 h. Reaction conditions: ZVI material =2.0 g/L, [p-NP]0 = 50.0 mg/L, pH0 5.0, T =25 °C, rotary rate =25 rpm, under anaerobic condition.
Illustration of the mechanisms of the enhanced p-NP reduction by C-dots@ZVI.

head.ce:title: Enhanced reduction of p-nitrophenol by zerovalent iron modified with carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resources Reuse
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pollution of aromatic nitro compounds in water is a critical issue, and the reduction of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) by the combination of catalyst/reductant has been a hot topic in recent years. The methods reported previously bore several defects, such as high cost for material synthesis and secondary pollution to the environment. Herein, this study employed the non-toxic and inexpensive materials, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) as the catalyst and zerovalent iron (ZVI) as the reductant, to prepare the ZVI modified with C-dots (C-dots@ZVI) for p-NP removal in water under anaerobic condition. The results showed that the removal rate of p-NP, the capacity, and the selectivity of ZVI towards p-NP removal were greatly improved, due to the involvement of C-dots. The rate constant of p-NP removal by C-dots@ZVI was 1192.7 folds of that by ZVI. The capacity of p-NP removal by C-dots@ZVI at 24.0 h was 24.8 orders of magnitude higher than that of ZVI. At the end of 2.0 h reaction with p-NP, the utilization ratio and electron efficiency of C-dots@ZVI were increased by 3.7 times and 2.9 times, respectively, compared to those of ZVI. The enhancing effect of C-dots on p-NP sequestration by ZVI should be mainly associated with the promoted hydrophobicity of ZVI surface, the facilitated electron transfer, and the accelerated corrosion of ZVI. As an innovative catalyst/reductant system for p-NP reduction, C-dots@ZVI is effective, facile, low-cost, and environmental-friendly.

ce:section.ce:para: Aromatic nitro compounds have been widely used in the industrial production [1]. Among them, p-nitrophenol (p-NP) is a typical precursor and intermediate in the manufacture of many industrial products, such as dyes, explosives, herbicides, insecticides, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, and plasticizers [2,3]. Because of its high solubility and non-biodegradability, p-NP has been widely spread in the environment [4,5]. Furthermore, p-NP has acute toxicity on aquatic organisms and human beings, and has been reported to be a potential carcinogen [6,7]. Thus, p-NP has been listed as a priority pollutant and its concentration has been restricted to below 10 ng/L in water by US Environmental Protection Agency since 1979 [8]. Due to the electron-withdrawing effect of nitro group on the aromatic ring, p-NP shows resistance to conventional wastewater treatment technology, including chemical oxidation and biological degradation [9]. The reduction of p-NP to p-aminophenol (p-AP) is a suitable pre-treatment process, for the facts that p-AP is less toxic and more biodegradable compared to p-NP. In addition to the environmental significance, the transformation of nitro to amino functional group is also of great industrial importance because amines occupy a huge market portion in the chemical industry and nitro compounds are crucial raw materials for anilines and paracetamol production [1,10,11]. Therefore, the catalytic reduction of p-NP to p-AP has been extensively studied.

Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is generally used as the reducing agent in a large number of studies for the catalytic reduction of p-NP, and a multitude of catalysts were reported to be effective to catalyze the reduction of p-NP by NaBH4 [12]. Nonetheless, the high price of NaBH4 restricts its large-scale application, and a large number of salts as byproducts (e.g., sodium metaborate) are produced when NaBH4 is applied [1]. In the past two decades, zerovalent iron (ZVI) has drawn considerable attention as a moderately strong reducing agent for sequestering various oxidative contaminants in water, owning to its non-toxicity and low cost [13]. The nitro compounds can be reduced to biodegradable amines by ZVI, and ZVI has acceptable environmental impact when it is involved in the following biochemical processes [1,14–17]. However, the commercially available ZVI usually has low reactivity due to its intrinsic passive layer on the surface, which consists of iron (hydr)oxides generated in the manufacturing process [13]. Consequently, various countermeasures have been developed to enhance the reactivity of ZVI towards contaminants removal for groundwater remediation and wastewater treatment [13,18,19]. Noble metal combined with ZVI [20] and nanoscale ZVI [21] had been adopt to reduce p-NP in water, but the defects (such as high cost and potential ecotoxicity) limited the application of these methods. Electrolysis [22] and ultrasonic irradiation [23] were applied to assist ZVI for p-NP reduction in water, but the large amount of energy input made these methods impractical. Moreover, the efficiency of electrons donated by ZVI applied in the reductive sequestration of contaminants should be taken into account [24,25], which was rarely evaluated in the previous studies on p-NP reduction by ZVI.

Besides the reducing agents, catalysts are crucial for the catalytic reduction of p-NP. Nowadays, the most prevalent topic of p-NP reduction in water is using heterogeneous nanoscale catalyst, which usually consists of noble metals, to increase the reaction rate [26,27]. The reduction of p-NP to p-AP catalyzed by the noble metal universally achieves favorable rates and efficiencies at room temperature. However, the noble metal catalysts suffer from the high material costs and bear potential environmental risks. For instance, these noble metals and some additives applied in the process of catalysts synthesis have toxicity [1]. Carbon, an essential element of the organism, always plays an important role in human society, and carbon-based materials have attracted great attention because of the abundance of the raw materials, environmental-friendly impact, and excellent performance [28,29]. Various carbon-based materials, e.g., activated carbon [30], biochar [31], and carbon nanotube [32,33], have been employed to couple with ZVI for contaminants degradation in water, owing to their effects on alleviating the passivation/aggregation of ZVI and enhancing the enrichment/adsorption of contaminants. Nevertheless, these carbon materials, commonly used as support of ZVI, were of a relatively large size and hardly played a role in catalyzing the reaction. In addition, most of them had high material cost and complicated synthesis process. Carbon quantum dots (C-dots), a rising star of carbon material family, were first discovered in 2004 [34]. C-dots own a variety of superior properties and have gain great interest for their potential application in many areas, such as bio-imaging, medicines, optoelectrical energy, photocatalysis, printing, and sensing [35]. In the field of environmental research, compared to the inorganic nanomaterials, especially the metal nanomaterials, C-dots have many advantages like negligible-toxicity, high solubility, great biocompatibility, and chemical inertness [36,37]. Various methods have been adopted to fabricate C-dots, and the one-step hydrothermal method using economical and readily available carbon compounds as the raw material is a facile route [35,36]. With these promising advantages, C-dots show the potential to replace metal nanomaterials for catalytic degradation of contaminants in environmental applications. Compared to the other carbon-based materials, C-dots have the advantages of facile preparation and catalytic reactivity.

In sum, an economical and environmental-friendly catalyst/reductant system is in need for effective reduction of p-NP, and the selectivity of the reductant should be examined. In this study, we selected C-dots as the catalyst and ZVI as the reductant, to synthesize ZVI modified with C-dots (C-dots@ZVI) for the catalytic reduction of p-NP. A comprehensive investigation of C-dots@ZVI with regard to its reaction rate, electron efficiency, and capacity for p-NP sequestration was conducted, and the mechanism of enhanced p-NP reduction by C-dots@ZVI was explored in detail.

The pristine ZVI sample (referred as ZVI in the following) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The median particle size (d50) of ZVI is 40.5 μm and its specific surface area is 0.15 m2/g. The detailed properties of ZVI sample used in this study is shown in Table S1 of the Supporting information (SI). All chemicals employed in this study, including p-NP (J&K Scientific Ltd.), p-AP (Qiangshun Chemical Reagent Co.), and the other agents (Qiangshun Chemical Reagent Co.), were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The deoxygenated deionized water was prepared by purging Milli-Q water with N2 for 1.0 h before left in the anaerobic chamber overnight, and all solutions in this study were prepared with deoxygenated deionized water in the anaerobic chamber (Mikrouna, N2-filld glove box, O2 < 0.8 ppm). Using citric acid as the raw material, C-dots were prepared with a hydrothermal method modified from the previous studies [38,39]. C-dots@ZVI was prepared by mixing the C-dots solution and the ZVI particles through rotary evaporation in vacuum. The fabrication procedure of C-dots and C-dots@ZVI is schematically presented in Fig. 1 , and the corresponding description of the detailed procedure is shown in Text S1. ZVI modified with citric acid (referred as citric-ZVI) and ZVI aged in water (referred as aged-ZVI) were synthesized with the same method of C-dots@ZVI preparation for the same duration but the C-dots solution was replaced with the citric acid solution (0.2 M, adjusted to pH 6.0 by NH3·H2O) and the ultrapure water of the same volume, respectively. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of ZVI, C-dots@ZVI, citric-ZVI, and aged-ZVI are shown in Fig. S1. The simple mixing of C-dots and ZVI particles is referred as C-dots/ZVI. When it comes to the discussion about the ratio of C-dots and ZVI, these materials were named as 1%C-dots/ZVI, 2%C-dots/ZVI, and 5%C-dots/ZVI to denote the mass ratios of C to ZVI dosed into p-NP solution. In comparison, the ratio values in the names of 1%C-dots@ZVI, 2%C-dots@ZVI, and 5%C-dots@ZVI indicate the mass ratios of C-dots to ZVI before the synthesis procedure of C-dots@ZVI. If it was not otherwise specified, the mass ratio of C to ZVI for both C-dots/ZVI and C-dots@ZVI was 5%.

All kinetic experiments were conducted under anaerobic condition. In the batch experiments, 0.1 g ZVI material (for example, C-dots@ZVI) was dosed into a vial containing 50 mL p-NP solution of 50.0 mg/L under anaerobic condition and then the vial was sealed with Teflon-lined septa. Hereafter, the vials were placed on the rotator (Hualida, WH-962C) with the rate of 25 rpm and the plane oriented 60° from horizontal, in a constant temperature incubator (Yuejin, SPX-GB) at 25 °C. At selected time intervals, 1 mL N2 was injected into the vial and then 1 mL liquid sample was withdrawn under anaerobic condition. The liquid sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and analyzed immediately. Blank control experiments and control experiments with C-dots alone were performed in parallel. Each batch test was performed in duplicate, and error bars in the figures show the standard deviations of test results. After batch tests, the precipitates were washed with deoxygenated deionized water, collected by suction filtration with 0.22 μm membrane filter, freeze-dried in vacuum for 48.0 h, and preserved in a N2-filled glovebox for subsequent characterization. To investigate the performance of C-dots@ZVI for p-NP removal in continuous operation and examine its reusability and capacity for p-NP removal, 5 consecutive runs of p-NP sequestration by one batch of C-dots@ZVI were carried out under anaerobic condition. The first run was initiated by adding 0.1 g C-dots@ZVI into 50 mL p-NP solution of 50.0 mg/L. The vial was sealed and rotated following the procedures of the batch test. When p-NP was completely removed, the working solution was dumped carefully and the solid particles were remained in the vial. A new working solution containing 50.0 mg/L p-NP with the volume of 50 mL was added into the vial and mixed with the particles, indicating that the next run was initiated.

The diluted C-dots solution was detected with the GC–MS system (Shimadzu QP2020) equipped with a purge-and-trap instrument (Tekmar Lumin) to analyze the constituents qualitatively. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of C-dots solution were determined with a steady/transient state fluorescence spectroscopy (Hitachi, F-4600). The image of C-dots was obtained with a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20). The contents of carbon in the pristine and modified ZVI materials were measured by the carbon-sulfur analyzer (ELTRA CS800). The SEM images and the elemental distributions of the samples were collected with a Hitachi 4700 microscope equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) at 15 kV. The EDS mapping of C-dots@ZVI was characterized with a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Zeiss Sigma HD). The analysis of carbon species on the surface of samples was carried out with X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB 250Xi). Fe K-edge extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) analyses were performed at the BL11B beamline at the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility (SSRF, Shanghai, China) to quantify the fractions of different iron (hydr)oxides in the solid phase, and the details are summarized in Text S2. The concentrations of p-NP and p-AP were examined by an ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC, H-class, Waters) equipped with a C18 column and an ultraviolet detector at the wavelength of 270 nm. At selected time intervals, pH of the solution was measured by dripping on the pH test strips. The concentration of dissolved Fe2+ was determined with the 1,10-phenanthroline colorimetric method using an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Purkinje TU-1902) at the wavelength of 510 nm. The concentrations of total soluble Fe were determined with an inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, Agilent, 720ES). The contents of Fe°, Fe2+, and Fe3+ in the solid phase of pristine and reacted samples were determined by a digestion method, and the details are elaborated in Text S3. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) analysis of electrodes decorated with different ZVI materials were performed to study their electrochemical properties. The electrochemical tests were conducted with a CHI618E electrochemical work station (Chenhua Instruments), and the detailed procedure is shown in Text S4. The water contact-angle measurements were conducted with a contact-angle meter (Kruss DSA, JY-82B), and the details are elaborated in Text S5.

According to the characterization results with GC–MS, the C-dots solution prepared in this study mainly contained small molecular ketones, such as acetone and cyclopentanone, verifying that synthesizing of C-dots with a hydrothermal method is a process of cracking and carbonization of organic matters to inorganic carbon matters and small organic molecules [39,40]. Photoluminescence behavior, a common property of C-dots, was observed when the synthesized C-dots solution was irradiated by visible and ultraviolet lights, as shown in the insets of Fig. 2 a [41]. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of C-dots solution were collected and shown in Fig. 2a, demonstrating the photoluminescence behavior of C-dots at the excitation wavelength of 320–420 nm. The synthesized C-dots have spherical shape with diameters ranging from 5 to 10 nm (Fig. 2b). The crystalline nature of C-dots examined by the high-resolution TEM clearly presented a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) facet of graphite (see inset in Fig. 2b) [42,43]. The above characterization results verified the C-dots were successfully synthesized and were uniform, well-dispersed, and of high crystallinity.

Quantified with carbon-sulfur analyzer, the weight fraction of carbon contained in pristine ZVI was determined to be 0.002%, while those of 1%C-dots@ZVI, 2%C-dots@ZVI, and 5%C-dots@ZVI were 0.601%, 1.122%, and 2.873%, respectively. These results suggest that the carbon from C-dots were successfully bound to ZVI particles. The carbon content in C-dots@ZVI was lower than the theoretical value, indicating that not all C-dots were incorporated into ZVI particles and the washing procedure caused the loss of carbon. As shown in Table S2, no obvious carbon signal was observed on the surface of pristine ZVI, whereas the weight fraction of carbon achieved 31.22% on the surface of C-dots@ZVI, detected by EDS-SEM. The maximum depth of EDS sampling was 5 μm, while the diameter of ZVI particles applied was 40.5 μm. Therefore, it can be concluded that the majority of carbon existed on the surface of C-dots@ZVI. EDS mapping of C-dots@ZVI (Fig. 2c) reveals that C was evenly distributed on the surface, and O may come from iron oxides and C-dots. XPS spectra of C 1s for ZVI and C-dots@ZVI were used to further unveil the C-dots combined in the ZVI particle (Fig. 2d). The peaks located at 284.6 eV for both samples corresponded to the CC bond with the sp2 orbital, which were ascribed to the graphite using as the background of XPS test. The peak at 286.0 eV was assigned to the CO bond, which may be attributed to the carbon contamination from the environment [44]. For the C 1s XPS spectrum of C-dots@ZVI, the peak centered at the binding energy of 287.3 eV belonged to the CO bond. The CO bond in the C-dots@ZVI sample came from the small molecular ketones, indicating the presence of C-dots in the composite [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of C-dots@ZVI for p-NP reduction
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetics of p-NP abatement by C-dots, pristine ZVI, citric-ZVI, aged-ZVI, C-dots/ZVI, and C-dots@ZVI are presented in Fig. 3 a. Apparently, there was almost no removal of p-NP in the groups of blank (only p-NP solution) and C-dots alone. The sequestrations of p-NP by ZVI and citric-ZVI proceeded slowly. After 48.0 h, 20.3% and 25.1% of p-NP were sequestrated by ZVI and citric-ZVI, respectively. Compared to those by ZVI and citric-ZVI, higher removal efficacy of p-NP (55.8%) was achieved by aged-ZVI in 48.0 h. Application of C-dots/ZVI achieved complete p-NP removal in 6.0 h, and the disappearance rate of p-NP by C-dots@ZVI was higher, resulting in a complete removal of p-NP in 2.0 h.

In addition to those by 5%C-dots/ZVI and 5%C-dots@ZVI, the kinetics of p-NP removal by C-dots/ZVI and C-dots@ZVI at C/ZVI ratio of 1% and 2% were further investigated, and the results are presented in Fig. S2a. The kinetics of p-NP disappearance were simulated with pseudo-first-order rate law (Fig. S3), and the obtained rate constants (k obs) were summarized in Fig. 3b. The rate of p-NP sequestration by ZVI and citric-ZVI were very small, while the rate constant of p-NP removal by aged-ZVI was increased to 0.020 h−1. Both the dosing of C-dots (C-dots/ZVI) and combining C-dots with ZVI (C-dots@ZVI) could obviously accelerate p-NP sequestration, and the values of k obs could be ranked in order of 1%C-dots/ZVI < 1%C-dots@ZVI < 2%C-dots/ZVI < 2%C-dots@ZVI < 5%C-dots/ZVI < 5%C-dots@ZVI. It should be noted that the mass ratio of C to ZVI before synthesis is used to name C-dots@ZVI, while the mass ratio of C to ZVI for C-dots/ZVI is exactly that of C-dots/ZVI dosing into p-NP solution. In other words, using the same named ratio, the actual mass ratio of C to ZVI for C-dots@ZVI was much less than that for C-dots/ZVI. Overall, the rate constants of p-NP reduction by ZVI increased with increasing mass ratio of C to ZVI, and the p-NP reduction rate of C-dots@ZVI was always higher than that of C-dots/ZVI at the same mass ratio of C/ZVI used for materials synthesis. The rate constant of p-NP removal by 5%C-dots@ZVI was up to 3.578 h−1, which was 1193-folds of that by ZVI.

It is well documented that the NO2 group on organics can be reduced by Fe0 and the final product is exclusively NH2 group [14,15,46]. To testify that p-NP are removed mainly by reduction to p-AP, the working solution during reaction and the solid phase were further examined. As shown in Figs. 3c and S2b, the main product of p-NP reduction by these ZVI materials was p-AP, and the concentration of p-AP in C-dots@ZVI system ascended most rapidly. Figs. 3d and S2c illustrate the variation of the total concentration of p-NP and p-AP with time in solution, indicating that partial contaminants were adsorbed in the solid phase. It was found that no p-NP existed in the solid precipitates and p-AP was the major reductive product of p-NP after the reaction of p-NP with different ZVI materials for 2.0 h (Table S3). Therefore, it can be concluded that the reduction of p-NP to p-AP is the major mechanism of p-NP removal by ZVI, and partial p-AP can be incorporated into the iron (hydr)oxides arising from ZVI corrosion. Upon the involvement of C-dots, the reduction of p-NP by ZVI was greatly accelerated and the amount of reduced p-NP was considerably increased, whereas the mechanism of p-NP reduction by ZVI was not significantly altered. The sequestration of p-NP by C-dots@ZVI proceeded with the largest rate, and the contaminant removed from solution consisted primarily of the adsorbed p-AP in the solid phase.

After determining the enhancing effect on C-dots on the reactivity of ZVI towards p-NP reduction with batch tests, the reusability of C-dots@ZVI was examined in consecutive runs and the capacity of C-dots@ZVI for p-NP removal was evaluated. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , compared to ZVI, C-dots@ZVI kept an outstanding performance for p-NP sequestration in four runs, until Fe° was exhausted in the fifth run within 24.0 h (determined with the digestion method and the data not shown). The kinetics of p-NP removal by C-dots@ZVI in each run could be well simulated with pseudo-first-order rate law. Although the rate constant of p-NP removal by C-dots@ZVI in four runs decreased gradually form 3.11 h−1 to 0.56 h−1, which was still greater than the rate constant of p-NP removal by C-dots/ZVI (0.44 h−1), indicating that C-dots@ZVI in recycle runs had the higher reactivity than C-dots/ZVI until Fe° was exhausted. Therefore, it can be concluded the main cause for the decreasing reactivity of C-dots@ZVI in recycle runs was the consumption of ZVI rather than the loss of C-dots. Moreover, the capacity of p-NP removal by C-dots@ZVI at 24.0 h was determined to be 101.7 mg/g, which was nearly 25 orders of magnitude higher than that of ZVI (4.1 mg/g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the enhanced p-NP reduction by C-dots@ZVI
ce:section.ce:para: The enhancing effect of C-dots for p-NP reduction by ZVI may be associated with three reasons. Firstly, C-dots could improve the hydrophobicity of ZVI surface and thus promote the enrichment of contaminants. The water contact-angle of each ZVI material was examined three times (Fig. S4) and the results were summarized in Fig. 5 a. Obviously, the addition of C-dots promoted the hydrophobicity of ZVI surface, and the water contact-angle of C-dots@ZVI increased with C/ZVI ratio increasing from 1% to 5%.

Secondly, C-dots could facilitate the process of electron transfer. Fig. S5 depicted the Tafel plots of the LSV obtained with the working electrode modified with different ZVI materials, and the corresponding parameters derived from fitting of these plots was shown in Fig. 5b and Table S4. The processes of Tafel curves fitting and parameters calculation were elaborated in Text S4. As graphed in Fig. 5b, the corrosion potentials (E corr) of ZVI, citric-ZVI, and aged-ZVI were in the range of −0.404–−0.432 V, whereas that of C-dots@ZVI ranged from −0.575 to −0.659 V, indicating the involvement of C-dots lowered the electrode potential of ZVI by 140–220 mV. The corrosion current density (i corr) of 1%C-dots@ZVI was 0.143 mA/cm2, which was higher than those of ZVI, citric-ZVI, and aged-ZVI. As the C/ZVI ratio was elevated to 2% and 5%, the i corr of C-dots@ZVI was further increased to 0.225 mA/cm2 and 0.347 mA/cm2, respectively. Compared to the polarization resistance (R p) of ZVI, the R p of citric-ZVI was ascended and the R p of aged-ZVI was descended, which should mainly be ascribed to the surface passivation caused by citric acid and the iron (hydr)oxides generated on the surface of aged-ZVI. Notably, the R p of C-dots@ZVI was declined and the values of R p dropped with increasing the ratio of C/ZVI, which revealed the more distinguished property of C-dots@ZVI for electron transfer than that of the other ZVI materials. To verify the enhancing effect of C-dots for electron transfer, EIS of ZVI and C-dots@ZVI were measured at open circuit potential mode. The Nyquist plots and corresponding fitted curves as well as the equivalent circuit are shown in Fig. S6. It should be noted R ct in the equivalent circuit represented the resistance of charge-transfer through the solid-electrolyte interphase film [47]. Compared to that of ZVI (4589 Ω/cm2), the R ct values of 1%C-dots@ZVI (891 Ω/cm2), 2%C-dots@ZVI (801 Ω/cm2), and 5%C-dots@ZVI (746 Ω/cm2) were lower, suggesting the accelerated electron transfer induced by C-dots.

Thirdly, C-dots could accelerate the corrosion of ZVI. As summarized in Table S3, the amounts of Fe° consumption after p-NP removal by different ZVI materials in 2.0 h could be ranked in the order of 5%C-dots@ZVI > 5%C-dots/ZVI > 2%C-dots/ZVI > 2%C-dots@ZVI > 1%C-dots@ZVI > 1%C-dots/ZVI > aged-ZVI > ZVI > citric-ZVI. As shown in Fig. S1, the corrosions of C-dots/ZVI and C-dots@ZVI were evidently more drastic than that of ZVI, after 48.0 h reaction with p-NP solution. As shown in Fig. S7, compared to the other groups, the groups with the applications of C-dots/ZVI and C-dots@ZVI experienced the faster increase of pH values and Fe2+ concentrations, which verified the accelerated corrosion of ZVI caused by the involvement of C-dots. To identify the corrosion products of ZVI and C-dots@ZVI before and after the reaction with p-NP, the Fe K-edge EXAFS spectra were recorded and the linear combination fitting (LCF) analysis was conducted (Fig. 5c). It was found that the fraction of Fe° in the solid phase of both ZVI at 0 h and ZVI at 2.0 h were ∼100%. It demonstrated that the amount of ferric (hydr)oxides generated in the ZVI sample after 2.0 h under anaerobic condition was minor, which could not be revealed by the LCF results of Fe k 3-weighted EXAFS spectra. Similarly, the pristine C-dots@ZVI was consisted of ∼100% Fe°, according to the LCF result of its Fe k 3-weighted EXAFS spectrum, indicating that negligible corrosion products were generated in the process of C-dots@ZVI synthesis. By contrast, 88% Fe and 12% γ-Fe2O3 were detected in the reacted C-dots@ZVI sample at 2.0 h, which suggested the elevated corrosion of ZVI caused by the involvement of C-dots. These EXAFS results were consistent with the data of the molar concentrations of Fe with various valence states determined with digestion method (Table S3). To further verify the effect of accelerated ZVI corrosion on the enhanced removal of p-NP/p-AP, some adsorption experiments were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. S8. There was negligible removal of p-NP by γ-Fe2O3, and the removal percentage of p-AP by γ-Fe2O3 and by ZVI at 48.0 h was 10.2% and 13.4%, respectively. It can be concluded the adsorption capacity of γ-Fe2O3 for p-NP/p-AP is relatively low, and p-AP removal by ZVI is limited by the slow corrosion of ZVI under anaerobic condition. As shown in Fig. S8, it was found that p-AP removal by C-dots@ZVI was at a relatively high rate and the removal percentage of p-AP by C-dots@ZVI at 48.0 h attained 51.4%. p-AP can be incorporated into the inner structure of the freshly generated iron (hydr)oxides during its removal by C-dots@ZVI, which is different from the commercially available γ-Fe2O3.

To better examine the utilization and selectivity of ZVI towards p-NP reduction, two parameters are introduced as following. Utilization ratio (UR) is defined as the fraction of the consumed ZVI (the electrons of primarily Fe° and secondly Fe2+) at given reaction time [48,49]. Electron efficiency (EE), describes the fraction of electrons transmitted to the target contaminant over the amounts of electrons provided by ZVI, had been used to assess the selectivity of ZVI for reductive removal of the target contaminant [48–50]. Due to the extremely low amount of the other reaction products, only the electrons transferred for reducing p-NP to p-AP were taken into account, for the purpose of simplifying the calculation. Thus, the values of UR and EE of different ZVI materials can be calculated via Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.
(1)
U R ( % ) =

F e
I I
Where nFe(III), nFe(II), and n p
-AP are the molar quantities of Fe(III), Fe(II), and p-AP in both solid and liquid phases at given reaction time, respectively, and n’Fe(III), n’Fe(II), and n’Fe(0) stand for the molar quantities of Fe(III), Fe(II), and Fe(0), respectively, in both solid and liquid phases before the reaction. Table S3 provides the molar concentrations of Fe with various valence states and p-AP, before and after two hours of p-NP removal by different ZVI materials. Based on these data, the values of UR and EE were calculated, and the results were summarized in Table S3 and were depicted in Fig. 5d. Both the UR and EE of ZVI were relatively low (4.3% and 6.3%), and the UR of aged-ZVI was enhanced to 8.0% but the EE of aged-ZVI dropped to 4.0%. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the particles of ZVI, citric-ZVI, and C-dots@ZVI present similarly with smooth surface, while the surface of aged-ZVI became coarse before reaction with p-NP. In accordance with the data in Table S2, the weight fraction of O on the surface of aged-ZVI was 14.76%, which was much higher than those of the other ZVI materials, suggesting the pre-corrosion of aged-ZVI. Pre-corrosion products on the surface of aged-ZVI favored the adsorption of contaminants, and the pre-corroded ZVI was more susceptible to be oxidized subsequently in solution [51], resulting in the increased p-NP removal and UR but decreased EE (Figs. 3a and 5 d), compared to those of ZVI. Compared to that of ZVI, the UR of citric-ZVI was slightly declined to 3.8%, which may be attributed to the passivation of the particle surface caused by the introduction of citric acid, and the EE of citric-ZVI was mildly improved to 7.1%. Compared to that of ZVI, the water contact-angle of citric-ZVI was larger while that of aged-ZVI was smaller (Fig. 5a), which may contribute to the higher EE of citric-ZVI and higher UR of aged-ZVI than those of ZVI. The increased hydrophobicity of citric-ZVI should be attributed to the passivation layer formed on the surface [52,53], and the decreased hydrophobicity of aged-ZVI should be ascribed to the generation of loose iron (hydr)oxides on the surface [54]. The UR and EE of C-dots/ZVI and C-dots@ZVI were superior to those of ZVI, and the enhancing effect on the UR and EE was improved as a function of C/ZVI ratio. At the same named C/ZVI ratio, C-dots/ZVI and C-dots@ZVI bore almost identical values of UR, but the EE value of C-dots@ZVI was always higher than that of C-dots/ZVI. At the end of 2.0 h reaction with p-NP, the values of UR and EE of 5%C-dots@ZVI reached 15.7% and 18.0%, which were increased by 3.7 times and 2.9 times, respectively, compared to those of ZVI.

Fig. 6 schematically illustrated the mechanisms of the enhanced p-NP reduction by C-dots@ZVI. C-dots could promote the hydrophobicity of particle surface [55,56], and thereby the enrichment of hydrophobic contaminants could be facilitated. Furthermore, C-dots could facilitate the process of electron transfer by reducing the R p of C-dots@ZVI [57–59], resulting in a more easily reduction of p-NP to p-AP. Moreover, C-dots combined on the surface of ZVI could accelerated the corrosion of ZVI. Subsequently, the passive layer could be destroyed readily to expose more Fe0 in the core, which could participate in the reduction of p-NP, and the adsorption of contaminants could be favored by the newly formed iron (hydr)oxides [13,48,49]. In general, C-dots could improve the hydrophobicity of ZVI surface, the electron transfer between ZVI and contaminants, and the corrosion rate of ZVI, leading to the larger rate, the higher capacity, and the more favorable electron selectivity of C-dots@ZVI towards p-NP reduction, compared to those of ZVI.

This study revealed that C-dots could significantly accelerate the reduction of p-NP by ZVI. The rate constant of p-NP reduction by C-dots@ZVI was increased with increasing the ratio of C/ZVI from 1% to 5%. When the C/ZVI mass ratio was identical for synthesizing C-dots@ZVI and dosing C-dots/ZVI, the p-NP reduction rate of C-dots@ZVI was always higher than that of C-dots/ZVI, indicating combining C-dots and ZVI has the coupled effect for enhanced reduction of p-NP. In the experiments for evaluating the reusability of C-dots@ZVI, the capacity of C-dots@ZVI for p-NP removal was evaluated, which was increased by ∼25 times compared to that of ZVI. The mechanisms of the enhanced reduction of p-NP by C-dots@ZVI were investigated in detail. Therefore, taking advantage of C-dots combined on the surface of ZVI, we developed a novel strategy to significantly enhance the reactivity, capacity, UR, and EE of ZVI towards p-NP sequestration. Compared to the other methods of enhancing reductants for p-NP reduction, this approach is cost-effective, environmental-friendly, innovative, and simple. This work provides a new way to combine the non-toxic and economical catalyst and reductant for removing the contaminants in water, which benefits the future application of the technologies for wastewater treatment and groundwater remediation. Nonetheless, there are some issues need to be addressed in future studies, such as the feasibility of employing C-dots@ZVI to remove multiple contaminants and to treat the practical wastewater.

Peng Fan: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Xuanjun Zhang: Resources. Huanhuan Deng: Resources. Xiaohong Guan: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22025601, 21777117, and U1932119), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFC1805202), and State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse Foundation (No. PCRRK20014). We thank beamline BL11B (Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility) for providing the beam time.
